contents: www.PRSJournal.com782elastic surgeons are judged by the scars--or lack thereof--that they produce, and patients seeking purely cosmetic procedures hold high standards with regard to postoperative scar aesthet-ics. Furthermore, unsightly scars may cause patients serious psychosocial distress in the perioperative period.1-3 Several therapies exist to treat various scar types, including gels, creams, chemotherapeutic injectables, laser, and pressure therapy.4-8 However, systematic review of the best evidence in preventa-tive scar management is seriously lacking, mainly because of the scarcity of clinical trial data with ade-quate controls. The following is a systematic review of current literature to critically evaluate scar care modalities based on the highest level of clinical trial data available to date.Our population includes patients with surgi-cal incisions with no known history of pathologic wound healing. The outcome of interest is aes-thetically acceptable scar healing (e.g., native skin match, minimal erythema, no hypertrophy). For the purpose of focusing this review, outcomes of therapeutic modalities for preexisting pathologic scarring (i.e., keloids) were excluded.A systematic review was conducted by means of computer-based MEDLINE search and Medical Subject Headings terms "cicatrix" AND "therapy, radiotherapy, surgery, drug therapy, prevention, and control." Abstracts and reference lists of the resultant articles were reviewed for inclusion and exclusion criteria as outlined (Table 1).Disclosure: Dr. Rohrich receives instrument royal-ties from Eriem Surgical, Inc., and book royalties from Thieme Medical Publishing. Neither author has any financial interest in any of the products or devices mentioned herein. Dr. Perez has no financial information to disclose. No funding was received for this article.Copyright (c) 2017 by the American Society of Plastic SurgeonsDOI: 10.1097/PRS.0000000000003894

Justin L. Perez, M.D.Rod J. Rohrich, M.D.Dallas, TexasBackground: Scar management is critical for every plastic surgeon's practice and, ultimately, the patient's satisfaction with his or her aesthetic result. De-spite the critical nature of this component of routine postoperative care, there has yet to be a comprehensive analysis of the available literature over the past decade to assess the best algorithmic approach to scar care. To this end, a sys-tematic review of best practices in preventative scar management was conduct-ed to elucidate the highest level of evidence available on this subject to date.Methods: A computerized MEDLINE search was performed for clinical studies addressing scar management. The resulting publications were screened random-ized clinical trials that met the authors' specified inclusion/exclusion criteria.Results: This systematic review was performed in May of 2016. The initial search for the Medical Subject Headings term "cicatrix" and modifiers "therapy, radio-therapy, surgery, drug therapy, prevention, and control" yielded 13,101 initial articles. Applying the authors' inclusion/exclusion criteria resulted in 12 rel-evant articles. All included articles are randomized, controlled, clinical trials.Conclusions: Optimal scar care requires taking into account factors such as inci-sional tension, anatomical location, and Fitzpatrick skin type. The authors pres-ent a streamlined algorithm for scar prophylaxis based on contemporary level I and II evidence to guide clinical practice. (Plast. Reconstr. Surg. 140: 782e, 2017.)From the Department of Plastic Surgery, University of  Texas Southwestern Medical Center; and the Dallas Plastic  Surgery Institute.Received for publication September 19, 2016; accepted April 3, 2017.Presented at the Cosmetic Bootcamp Meeting, in Aspen,  Colorado, July 7 through 10, 2016.

Optimizing Postsurgical Scars: A Systematic Review on Best Practices in Preventative  Scar ManagementSPECIAL TOPICCopyright (c) 2017 American Society of Plastic Surgeons. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. RESULTS

PubMed SearchThe computer-based MEDLINE search was conducted in May of 2016. Initial PubMed search [Medical Subject Headings (a vocabulary con-trolled by the National Library of Medicine to uniformly index biomedical literature) terms "cicatrix" AND "therapy, radiotherapy, surgery, drug therapy, prevention and control" modifiers] yielded 13,101 articles (Fig. 1). When filtered for clinical trials, 865 articles resulted. When "pub-lished within last 10 years," "human," "English," and "full text article" filters were applied, 438 arti-cles resulted. After abstract screening for inclu-sion/exclusion criteria, our MEDLINE search produced 18 articles of interest. Of these, six were excluded for experimental modalities with pre-liminary data.Study Characteristics Given our goal of critically analyzing the high-est level of therapeutic data, all included articles are randomized controlled trials. All studies address postsurgical scars within 1 year of surgery. Study endpoints are predicated on standardized, validated scar assessment tools (i.e., Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale, Patient and Observer Scar Assessment Scale, Manchester Scar Scale, or graded tiers of scar appearance) (Figs. 2 and 3).9-14Study ComparisonThe heterogeneous nature of outcome mea-sures in the selected studies precluded a meaning-ful numerical meta-analysis. Scar assessment tools, although an attempt to objectify scar appearance and morphology, are inherently descriptive.9-14 Thus, a narrative review of the available evi-dence was conducted (Tables 2 through 5).15-26 Selected clinical trials were divided into subgroups based on modality: topical therapy (keratin, silicone gel/sheets), laser therapeutics (pulsed dye laser, neodymium:yttrium-aluminum-garnet laser, 1550-nm nonablative laser, potassium-tit-anyl-phosphate laser), or compressive measures (mechanomodulation).

Topical Scar Management StudiesThree studies scrutinized silicone gel as topical means to optimize linear postsurgical scars.15,18,19 The first, by de Giorgi et al., compared use of silicone gel against zinc oxide cream controls.15 Outcome measures were resultant scar type (cat-egorized as physiologic versus pathologic) and wound healing symptoms (i.e., paresthesia, pull-ing sensation, erythema). Pathologic scarring (i.e., widened, atrophic, or hypertrophic) was lower in the treatment group (27 percent) compared with the zinc oxide group (55 percent). No keloids formed in the treatment group, versus 11 per-cent keloid formation in controls. Hypertrophic scarring was decreased in the treatment group (9  percent) compared with controls (22 percent). In sum, there was a 28 percent absolute reduction rate (51 percent relative reduction) in pathologic scarring, with a number needed-to-treat of 3.6 patients to prevent one "pathologic" scar. Silicone gel with sunscreen additive resulted in less scar hyperpigmentation than zinc oxide alone.Scuderi et al. and Signorini and Clementoni compared silicone gel (Dermatix) to either cya-noacrylate (Wipescar) or no treatment, respec-tively.18,19 In the former, profilometry showed equal scar elevation in both cohorts at 1 month (range, 0.176 to 0.177 mm) with equal scar flat-tening at 1 year (range, 0.122 to 0.125 mm). Der-matix scars were 75 percent wider (1.4 mm) versus Wipescar (0.8 mm; p < 0.0001). A trend toward increased Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale scores was noted in both cohorts. Patients preferred applying cyanoacrylate every 3 to 5 days compared with twice-daily silicone gel application. No patho-logic scarring occurred.18 Signorini and Clem-entoni reported higher normal scar maturation in the treatment group (67 percent) compared with nontreated controls (28 percent). Further-more, patients without scar care had higher rates of scar hypertrophy at 55 percent (versus treat-ment group, 23 percent). Patients who developed hypertrophy were transitioned to triamcinolone injection.19Jina et al. compared keratin gel to aqueous cream and reported worse scarring in their con-trol (Manchester Scar Scale score, 14.2) versus treatment cohort (Manchester Scar Scale score,

Volume 140, Number 6 * Preventative Scar Management

12.2).16 Higher Manchester Scar Scale score cor-relates with more color mismatch, shinier appear-ance, elevation, and firm texture. The authors do not further quantify scar topography.27Maher et al. examined use of silicone sheets (continuous wear, 6 months) on linear postsur-gical scars versus no treatment (bisected scar).17 Observers consistently rated improved scar pli-ability and pigmentation. However, patients noted similar improvement in scar thickness, relief, surface, width, and vascularity in both treated and untreated scars. Furthermore, there was no difference in pain or pruritus. Adverse effects were limited to minor skin irritation (20 percent).* Use of silicone gel improves scar matura-tion with regard to color and texture, and decreases pathologic scarring rate com-pared with zinc oxide cream or no scar treatment at all.* Cyanoacrylate may be more efficacious than silicone gel in preventing scar widening.* Keratin gel may soften and flatten postster-notomy scars; data in cosmetic cohorts are lacking.* According to recent trials, silicone sheets do not alter scar appearance from the per-spective of patients with a history of normal wound healing.Light and Laser TherapyOf three trials that address laser therapy for scar prophylaxis in mixed patient populations, two compare nonablative fractional laser to either no therapy or pulsed dye laser.21,22 Verhaeghe et al. report significantly improved scar profiles by Phy-sician Global Assessment Scales at 1 (p < 0.001) and 3 months (p < 0.001) postoperatively.21 Inci-sions and full-thickness skin grafts treated with nonablative fractional laser scored significantly closer to native skin in thickness (p = 0.001), relief (p = 0.001), and pliability (p = 0.002) as assessed by

Copyright (c) 2017 American Society of Plastic Surgeons. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery * December 2017blinded physicians. There was no long-term differ-ence in skin reflectance. Adverse events included erythema (67 percent) and rare but serious events such as vesicle or ulcer formation (4 percent). No long-term adverse events occurred.Tierney et al. compared 595-nm pulsed dye laser against 1550-nm nonablative fractional laser for scar prophylaxis using an institutionally cre-ated five-point assessment scale (i.e., surveying dyspigmentation, hypopigmentation, thickness, texture, overall cosmesis).22 The authors noted improvement in all five aesthetic parameters--pigmentary variation (64.2 percent improve-ment with nonablative fractional laser versus 45.8 percent with pulsed dye laser; p < 0.001), hypopigmentation (65 percent improvement ver-sus 0 percent; p < 0.001), thickness (86.6 percent improvement versus 45.8 percent; p < 0.001), tex-ture (73.3 percent versus 52.5 percent; p < 0.001), and overall aesthetic appearance (75 percent ver-sus 53 percent; p < 0.001). Eighty-three percent of

patients globally preferred nonablative fractional laser. Nonablative fractional laser was associated with more postprocedural pain, but this did not dissuade patients from continuing therapy. Tran-sient side effects included mild pain, erythema, and edema. No blistering occurred.Vas et al. compared pulsed dye laser/neodymium:yttrium-aluminum-garnet laser against no treatment and found unanimously improved scar parameters by Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale (i.e., pigmentation, vascularity, pliability, and scar height; p < 0.05).20 The group provides der-moscopic evidence (e.g., skin surface microscopy) for improved scar architecture--namely, narrower, flatter scars with decreased vascularity. No signifi-cant complications were reported.Two studies addressed preventative laser therapy in patients with Fitzpatrick skin type IV or higher.23,24 Choe et al. reported a statistically significant decrease in Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale on bisectionalized scars treated with 1550-nm fractional Copyright (c) 2017 American Society of Plastic Surgeons. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Volume 140, Number 6 * Preventative Scar Managementerbium-glass laser versus no treatment.24 Erbium-glass laser-treated scars scored better in vascularity, pigmentation, pliability, and height (Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale score, 1.52 versus 3.00; p = 0.03) by blinded dermatologists. No adverse events (i.e., herpes labialis or postinflammatory hyperpigmenta-tion) were noted. Lee et al. reported improved scar appearance after potassium titanyl phosphate laser versus no treatment (Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale score, 2.2 versus 5.75; p < 0.001), improved global assessment scores (3.1 versus 1.8; p < 0.01), and higher cosmetic patient satisfaction scores (3.1 versus 1.6; p < 0.001).23 Blistering was reported in one patient, who opted to continue treatment.

Data Summarized* Nonablative fractional laser improves texture and pliability of incisions and full-thickness grafts on the face and neck when compared to no treatment, albeit with a considerable short-term side-effect profile.* Nonablative fractional laser improves scar pigmentary variation, hypopigmentation, thickness, and texture to a greater extent when compared head-to-head against pulsed dye laser therapy, although skin type of most benefit was not specified.ReferenceModalityComparative  ModalityStudy  Size (n)Single vs.  Multi-Institutionalde Giorgi et al., 200915Silicone gel (with sunscreen con-tained, brand not specified)Zinc oxide cream110SingleJina et al., 201516Keratin gel (KeragelT)Aqueous cream20SingleMaher et al., 201217Silicone sheet (brand not speci-fied)Nothing/untreated10SingleScuderi et al., 201118Silicone gel (Dermatix; Valeant Pharmaceuticals International, Aliso Viejo, Calif.)Cyanoacrylate (Wipescar,  FaselS.r.l.,  Bologna, Italy)85MultiSignorini and  Clementoni, 200719Silicone gel (Dermatix)Nothing/untreated160SingleVas et al., 201420Sequential 585-nm PDL/Nd:YAG Laser (Cynergy, Cynosure)Nothing/untreated25SingleVerhaeghe et al., 201221 1540-nm NAFLNothing/untreated24SingleTierney et al., 200922595-nm PDL (Candela Corp Inc., Wayland, Mass.)1550-nm NAFL (erbium doped  fiber laser (Fraxel RS, Reliant  Technologies,  Mountain  View, Calif.)12SingleYun et al., 201023532-nm KTP Laser (plus silicone sheeting)Silicone sheeting28SingleChoe et al., 2009241550-nm fractional erbium-glass laser (MOSAIC, Lutronic Co., Seoul, Republic of Korea)Nothing/untreated27SingleLongaker et al., 201425Embrace advanced scar therapy device (Neodyne)Nothing36MultiLim et al., 201426EmbraceNothing [Steri-Strips (3M, St. Paul, Minn.) alone or no treatment]12Single

Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery * December 2017* Combined pulsed dye laser/neody-mium:yttrium-aluminum-garnet laser ther-apy starting on the day of suture removal is safe and effective in improving the qual-ity and cosmetic appearance of surgical  scars in patients with skin types I through  IV with an apparently safe side-effect profile.* Both 532-nm potassium-titanyl-phosphate laser and 1550-nm erbium laser are effective in improving scar appearance in high-risk ethnic groups (Fitzpatrick skin type >=IV).Compressive TherapyTwo randomized controlled trials investigated the potential benefit of the Embrace device (a silicone elastomeric dressing) in postoperative scar management.25,26 Longaker et al. reported a statistically significant improvement in pigmen-tation (p < 0.001), pliability (p < 0.001), relief/roughness (p = 0.006), and vascularity (p = 0.004) according to the Observer Scar Assessment Scale on Embrace-treated abdominoplasty scars at 12-month follow-up.25 Enrolled subjects rated Embrace-treated scars as significantly better than their nontreated side [e.g., irregularity (p = 0.03), thickness (p = 0.01), stiffness (p = 0.002), and over-all aesthetic appearance (p = 0.02) according to

the Patient Scar Assessment Scale). Photographs were reviewed by blinded board-certified plastic surgeons, and Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale scores were significantly improved for treated (2.9) compared with control scars (3.29; p = 0.02). No preferential benefit was noted by age, race, body mass index, or Fitzpatrick skin type after multivariate analysis.Lim et al. also studied the prophylactic role of mechanomodulation and reported significantly improved Vancouver Scar Assessment Scale scores on treated scars (3.78) compared with controls (5.58; p < 0.05) when assessing vascularity, thick-ness, pliability, and pigmentation.26 On patient satisfaction surveys, 100 percent were satisfied/very satisfied with overall improved scar cosme-sis; 90 percent would recommend treatment to others; and 90 percent would want the Embrace (Gurtner and Longaker developed the Embrace device) device to optimize scarring if they under-went another procedure in the future.* Mechanomodulation therapy with the Embrace device compared to standard or no postoperative dressing improves scar vas-cularity, thickness, pliability, and pigmenta-tion, with high patient satisfaction rates.ReferenceTime of Therapy  InitiationModality  Compatible  with Makeup?Study Design  (Level of Evidence)Study Periodde Giorgi et al., 200915At suture removalYesSingle-blind (observer), prospective, randomized, controlled (I)May-July of 2005Jina et al., 2015167 days postoperativelyYesSingle-blind, randomized, controlled (I)NSMaher et al., 201217<1 yr postoperativelyNoProspective, randomized, controlled (IIb)NS

DISCUSSIONThere Is Insufficient Evidence for Routine Silicone Sheeting in Patients with Normal  Wound HealingAlthough there is a wide array of research focusing on silicone sheets to treat hypertro-phic scarring, the utility of silicone sheeting in patients with apparently normal wound heal-ing is mixed.28-32 Contemporary data contained herein corroborate these mixed results.17 That is, observer scales demonstrated some benefit in scar softness and pigmentation, but patients themselves saw little aesthetic improvement. The mechanistic benefit of silicone products is occlusion, which is hypothesized to normalize keratinocyte hydration and regulate extracellular matrix production.33 A potential confounder of consistent clinical results in the studies presented may be poor patient adherence to regular wear, potentially influenced by the need for prolonged wear, continued maintenance, or obtrusive appearance of sheeting.34,35 Furthermore, several well-marketed silicone sheets cater to lighter skin tones, and thus appear unnatural and unattract-ive on darker skin. As our assessment of the cur-rent data stands, silicone sheets may have a place among the techniques used to address pathologic scarring, but its routine, blanketed use in scar prophylaxis may be overzealous and costly.ReferenceScar TypeLocation of  ControlTotal  Follow-Up  Time (mo)Outcome Measure/ Scar Assessment  ToolAdverse  Effectsde Giorgi et al., 200915Linear surgicalDifferent scar (?) on same patient8 Resultant scar type (pathologic vs.  nonpathologic), wound healing symptomsJina et al., 201516SternotomySecond half of  sternotomy incision6 MSS score, POSASNone

Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery * December 2017There Is Evidence for Preventative Use of Silicone Gel in Mixed CohortsThe ideal first-line therapy for scar prophy-laxis should be effective, inconspicuous, and eas-ily applicable by patients themselves. As such, silicone gel is an appealing option, and the pre-sented data confirm that its use is compatible with other topical agents such as sunscreen, which have proven aesthetic benefits in postsurgical scar care to reduce hyperpigmentation.15,36-39 Although the clinical trials included did not stratify by Fitz-patrick skin type,15,18,19 the ubiquitous benefit obtained with regard to scar relief and vascularity suggests that silicone gel has universal appeal and should be considered in patients of all skin types regardless of whether or not they have a history of pathologic scarring.

There Is Evidence for Use of Cyanoacrylates for Extended Postoperative Scar Management in Cosmetic CohortsCyanoacrylates are commonly used in lieu of Steri-Strips or occlusive dressings on breast and abdominal incisions. Many surgeons consider this agent merely a convenient, temporary means to dress their surgical wounds. However, the clini-cal trial data presented assert an aesthetic benefit with prolonged application to decrease scar wid-ening.18 Mechanisms to account for this benefit may include resistance to traction and occlusion and antimicrobial properties.40-43 We propose that routine prolonged application of cyanoacrylates be considered in high-tension trunk or extrem-ity wounds, with regular application every 2 to 3 days for up to 2 to 3 weeks, which has been shown to be well-tolerated in patients undergoing aes-thetic procedures.18 Caution should be taken with repeated application of cyanoacrylates above the clavicle, as the polymerized product is poorly com-patible with makeup and thus gives a "caked-on" appearance in cosmetically sensitive areas.There Is Insufficient Evidence for the Routine Use of Keratin Gel in Cosmetic PatientsThe only clinical trial level evidence for use of keratin gel is in median sternotomy cohorts under-going cardiothoracic surgery.16 Although a plastic surgeon may be involved in sternal wound cover-age in complicated cases such as sternal wound breakdown or osteomyelitis, the reconstructive priorities in these cases would be stable closure and eradication of infection; thus, cosmetic scar appearance becomes a secondary or tertiary goal at best. Also, one should consider that keratin gel

is much less widely available in over-the-counter formulation than silicone gel, making silicone gel an arguably more preferred option. Until further clinical trial level evidence to support routine use of keratin gel in cosmetic cohorts is available, it should be considered a safe second-line alterna-tive if silicone gel is either poorly tolerated or unavailable. Of note, the evidence for prophylac-tic topical antioxidants (i.e., vitamin E) to improve the cosmetic outcome of surgical scars is weak; its use should be discouraged.44-46There Are No Clinical Trials to Support Routine Use of Steri-Strips in Addition to Suture Closure to Improve Cosmetic Appearance of ScarsAlthough there are few clinical trials that have studied Steri-Strip closure devices versus subcu-ticular closure,47,48 no clinical trial data exist to demonstrate an added cosmetic benefit of using Steri-Strips as a wound dressing in addition to suture closure.

There Is Evidence for Preventative Laser Therapy in Patients with High-Risk Fitzpatrick Skin TypesPatients with a Fitzpatrick skin type greater than or equal to 4 are at higher than average risk for pathologic scarring considering the genetic predisposition for keloid and hypertrophic scar-ring in darker skinned ethnic groups.49,50 How-ever, many surgeons are reluctant to use laser in ethnic patients, given the risk of postinflam-matory hyperpigmentation, which may worsen cosmetic outcome of scars.51-53 Few clinical trials have specifically addressed these high-risk groups and explored the potential aesthetic benefits of prophylactic laser therapy. The trials presented herein demonstrate with the highest level of evi-dence available that prophylactic laser therapy is not only cosmetically beneficial for head and neck scarring, but also safe (at least in Korean subjects).23,24 Further studies are needed to assess whether high-level cosmetic results and a low risk of postinflammatory hyperpigmentation can be recapitulated in other ethnic cohorts (e.g., His-panics, African Americans).There Is Limited Evidence That Nonablative Fractional Laser Is Superior for Scar Prophylaxis Compared with Pulsed Dye Laser, Although Several Laser Types Are EffectivePrior work has shown that pulsed dye laser, a commonly used nonablative laser, improves the appearance of raised postsurgical scars. However, Copyright (c) 2017 American Society of Plastic Surgeons. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Volume 140, Number 6 * Preventative Scar Management

recent data included in this review suggest that fractional laser resurfacing outperforms pulsed dye laser, particularly in repigmenting hypopig-mented scars.22 These results are not altogether surprising considering that fractional lasers have greater penetration depth and induce focal micro-thermal zones of injury in papillary and reticular dermis to stimulate collagen remodeling.54-56 It is also hypothesized that nonablative fractional laser repigments hypopigmented scars by stimulating the migration of normal melanocytes into resur-faced tissues.57 Overall, there are numerous laser types that have demonstrated efficacy at improv-ing scar dyspigmentation, thickness, texture,58 and overall cosmetic appearance and should be strongly considered by any plastic surgeon for scar prophylaxis, particularly on high-profile areas such as the neck and face.There Is Strong Evidence for Use of Mechanomodulatory Therapy on  High-Tension WoundsIn the largest, multi-institutional clinical trial contained in this systematic review, the Embrace Copyright (c) 2017 American Society of Plastic Surgeons. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery * December 2017device was shown to improve aesthetic scar quality after abdominoplasty at 1 year.25 The design of the device aims to offload tension, one of the most significant contributors to unattractive scar widen-ing or hypertrophy.25,26 Given that abdominoplasty closures are arguably the highest tension closures cosmetic surgeons face, the Embrace device is therefore well-suited for scar prophylaxis on trunk and extremity incisions and should be highly con-sidered in these cases. Furthermore, the device is widely available and well tolerated by wearers.

An Algorithmic ApproachOptimal scar care requires carefully approach-ing each patient individually, taking into account factors such as incisional tension, anatomical loca-tion, and Fitzpatrick skin type. A one-size-fits-all approach to postoperative incision care may be costly; yield suboptimal results; and predispose to scar widening, hypertrophy, or atrophy. We pro-pose a streamlined algorithm to guide surgeons toward evidence-based modalities for scar minimi-zation (Fig. 4).Study LimitationsThe main limitation of this study is a scarcity of randomized, controlled, clinical trials with regard to preventative scar therapy. Although there is a large volume of observational, case series, and case report data addressing postoperative scar optimization, the quality of clinical trials available for review is limited by short follow-up (<1 year), small cohorts, and heterogeneous patient popula-tions (e.g., acne cohorts plus burn cohorts with no distinction between the two). Importantly, all stud-ies included in this study were published within the past 10 years, thus emphasizing the need to stay current in scar care. This review valued qual-ity over quantity, with the goal of isolating the highest level of contemporary evidence with the strictest of inclusion and exclusion criteria of any scar care systematic review performed within the past 10 years. Still, more high-quality randomized controlled trials in scar prophylaxis are needed to further substantiate our claims.CONCLUSIONSThe synthesized results of this narrative sys-tematic review strongly support with level I and II evidence the use of topical therapies for aesthetic optimization of postoperative scar appearance, whether silicone gel, silicone sheet, or mechano-modulatory dressings with good patient tolerabil-ity and safe side-effect profiles. Our proposed scar

management algorithm provides plastic surgeons with an evidence-based approach to scar care rooted in data rather than sweeping subjective opinion.Rod J. Rohrich, M.D.9101 N Central Expressway, Suite 600Dallas, Texas REFERENCES
